4 min read
Just now

--

Landing a frontend role at a Big Four firm like KPMG requires more than just knowing how to build a basic UI. I recently went through the interview process for a ReactJS/Frontend Developer position, and the experience was incredibly eye-opening.
Instead of quizzing me on basic textbook definitions or syntax trivia, the interviewer zeroed in on what actually matters in production: writing efficient code, optimizing real-world application performance, and managing state at scale.
If you are preparing for a frontend loop at KPMG or a similar enterprise company, here is exactly what went down, the questions I faced, and the key takeaways you need to ace it.
📌 The Technical Breakdown: ReactJS & State Management
Large enterprise applications come with complex state and strict performance requirements. The React portion of the interview heavily reflected this, focusing on optimization and resource lifecycle management.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

1. Architectural & State Management Choices

• The Question: If you’re working on a large-scale B2B application, which state management solution would you choose and why?

• The Focus: The interviewer wanted to see if I understood the trade-offs between different architectures (e.g., Redux Toolkit for structured, predictable global state vs. Zustand/Recoil for lightweight, atomic state vs. React Context for simpler, deep prop-drilling fixes). The key here is justifying why a specific tool fits a large enterprise codebase.
2. Efficiency & Preventing Waste

• The Question: How can you prevent unnecessary API calls and avoid repeated re-rendering?

• The Focus: This targeted data-fetching efficiency. Discussing strategies like debouncing/throttling user actions, implementing caching layers (using tools like TanStack Query / RTK Query), and breaking down heavy components to isolate state changes.
3. Memory & Resource Cleanup

• The Question: How would you clear memory or clean up resources when a user navigates from one tab or page to another?

• The Focus: Garbage collection and memory management. They expected to hear about cleaning up event listeners, clearing active setInterval or setTimeout timers, and aborting ongoing HTTP requests using AbortController inside the useEffect cleanup return function.
4. Hook Optimization Micro-Patterns

• The Question: What is the difference between useMemo and useCallback?

• The Focus: Not just defining them, but explaining the performance cost of overusing them. useMemo memoizes a computed value, while callBack memoizes a function definition to prevent broken referential equality down the component tree.
5. Macro Performance Strategy

• The Question: What techniques would you use to improve the overall performance score of a React application?

• The Focus: A high-level architectural overview covering code-splitting via dynamic lazy imports, image optimization, minimizing bundle sizes, and analyzing rendering bottlenecks using the React DevTools Profiler.
📌 JavaScript & Problem-Solving: Beyond the Syntax
The coding portion wasn’t a generic LeetCode grind. The interviewer handed me an existing JavaScript snippet and asked me to critically analyze it.
The Challenge:
Identify the exact time complexity ($O(n)$, $O(n²)$, etc.) of the provided code.
Pinpoint the architectural bottleneck causing inefficiency.
Rewrite the solution from scratch with improved time and space complexity.
The expectations were high: they weren’t just looking for code that worked. They wanted clean, production-ready code accompanied by a clear, verbal explanation of my thought process and complexity analysis as I refactored the solution.
💡 My Key Takeaways for Your Preparation
Going through this loop made it clear that senior frontend engineering is no longer about just making things work — it’s about making things scale. If you are preparing for KPMG, structure your study plan around these pillars:

• Don’t Memorize, Internalize: Don’t just learn how to write a hook; understand the exact performance implications of when and why it re-renders.

• Master the Lifecycle: Be incredibly comfortable with resource cleanup. Leaking memory is one of the quickest ways to degrade enterprise web apps.

• Think Like a Performance Engineer: Practice looking at vanilla JavaScript code, calculating its Big O complexity on the fly, and refactoring nested loops into optimized data structures like Maps or Sets.

• Communicate Your Code: Speak out loud during the coding challenge. The interviewer cares just as much about how you navigate a problem as they do about the final line of code you write.
Good luck to everyone preparing for upcoming frontend interviews!
If you found this breakdown helpful, drop a clap 👏 and share your thoughts in the comments below.
